segments.s287-g209: که here introduces a benediction/wish ('may ...'); پیروز is an adjective 'victorious'. بادی is an archaic optative/subjunctive form (from بودن) meaning 'may you be' (classical Persian uses -ی to mark the wish). و simply links the two wished states ('and'); شاد 'happy/joyful'; آمدی is used in the optative sense 'may you come' (here the verb is understood as a wish rather than a simple past). Word order reflects older Persian: the wish particle + predicate before the verbal form.
segments.s287-g210: مبیناد is an archaic third-person subjunctive/optative meaning 'may ... appear' (here used with a negative sense introduced by هرگز that follows); چشم تو 'your eye' (possessed noun + pronoun); هرگز 'never' negates the following noun-phrase; بدی is a noun 'evil/harm'. The clause therefore reads literally 'may evil never appear to your eye.'
segments.s287-g211: همی is the classical durative/continuous marker that accompanies past verbs to give an iterative or ongoing sense ('kept on'); تاخت is the past (3rd sg.) 'galloped/rode swiftly'; بیژن is the subject (placed after the verb, a common classical order); پس 'then/afterward'; گستهم is an archaic 1st-person past form ('I went/ I followed' from گشتن) — together: 'Bijan rode on; I then followed.'
segments.s287-g212: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'); ناید is here optative/negative ('may not / should not come upon'); بر او 'upon him' (preposition + pronoun); the second بر combines with ز 'from' to mark source ('from Turan'); توران is the place-name 'Turan'; ستم 'oppression/injustice'. The whole clause: 'so that no oppression from Turan should come upon him.'
segments.s287-g41: که is the relative/nominalizing particle 'who/that' introducing a clause. جوید is the classical present/3rd-person-singular form 'seeks' (used here as a simple present). کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb modifying جوید; نام here means 'fame/reputation' and نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near the king' positioned after the noun it qualifies (typical flexible word order in poetry).
segments.s287-g42: بپوشد is a present/optative-type classical form meaning 'he puts on / he should put on' but here rendered as simple present 'puts on'. سرش is head + enclitic possessive 'his' (سر + ش), and را is the definite object marker marking 'his head' as the direct object. به رومی کلاه literally 'a/with a Roman hat' places the instrument/thing (کلاه) after the prepositional marker به and its qualifier رومی.
segments.s287-g43: This line inverts normal prose order for poetic effect: همه 'all' + predicate مانده بودند 'had remained' comes before the explicit subject ایرانیان 'the Iranians'. مانده بودند is a past/perfect compound (مانده + بودند) meaning 'had remained' or 'were left'.
segments.s287-g44: شده is the perfect/passive participial sense 'have become'. سست 'weak' is a predicative adjective; و simply links adjectives. سوده here is lexically unclear in the supplied senses (context suggests 'injured/worn' by iron), and is followed by the ablative/agentive ز 'from' plus آهن (poetic 'iron' = weapons/steel). میان 'amid/in the midst' indicates location. The whole phrase describes the Iranians as made weak and afflicted by iron in the fighting.
segments.s287-g45: ندادند is the negative past 'they did not give' (نه + دادند → negation expressed as a single verbal form here). پاسخ 'answer' is the direct object; جز از 'except from' introduces the sole source of any reply. گستهم here functions as a proper name (the only one who answered), not its literal lexical sense.
segments.s287-g46: که is the relative 'who/that' referring back to گستهم. بود is the past auxiliary 'was' and combines with آورد (past 'brought' or 'produced') to form 'had brought' or 'brought forth'. اندر is a poetic preposition 'in/within', and شیر دژم is a compound noun phrase 'a grim/sullen lion' functioning as the object of آورد describing what he had produced or presented.
segments.s287-g47: به سالار گفت places the indirect object first: 'he said to the commander' (به = to/for + سالار 'commander'). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses were for a noun; the function in context is the verbal past 'he said'). ای سرافراز شاه is a vocative phrase 'O exalted/lofty king' addressing the king directly.
segments.s287-g48: چو رفتی is a temporal clause 'when you went' (چو = 'when', رفتی = 2nd-person-past 'you went/you went off'). به آورد here uses به + infinitive-like construction with آورد 'to bring' meaning 'to bring/bring about', and توران سپاه 'the army of Turan' is the object (Turān as genitive modifier of سپاه). The clause begins reported speech introduced by the previous گفت.
segments.s287-g121: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'); the clause has poetic word order: 'با او بود یار' literally 'with him was (a) companion' (بود is past 'was'). 'گاه نبرد' is a compound nominal phrase 'the time/occasion of battle' (گاه = 'time', نبرد = 'battle'), modifying the kind of companion.
segments.s287-g122: This is a compact prepositional phrase: 'سر دشمنان' = 'the heads of the enemies' (سر = 'head'); 'اندر آرد' with آرد (present 'brings') means 'puts/brings into/among'; 'به گرد' functions together as 'around' (به + گرد). The verb آرد is third person singular present (poetic narrative) referring back to the companion.
segments.s287-g123: بدو here is the dative 'to him' ('to that person') and precedes گفت in the customary terse narrative order 'بدو گفت بیژن' = 'Bijan said to him'. The token گفت in the segment is the verb 'said' (past), but the provided sense-listing doesn't include a simple 'said' sense, so the sense index is set to -1. که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory form ('که ای پهلوان' = 'O champion'), archaic/poetic in function.
segments.s287-g124: A string of epithets describing the addressee: خردمند 'wise/prudent', و 'and' joins the adjectives, بیدار 'awake/alert', و 'and', روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled/cheerful'. These adjectives follow the vocative and are attributive to پهلوان; there is no separate verb in this segment.
segments.s287-g125: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. یار باید is an impersonal construction 'a companion is needed' (باید = 'it is necessary/one must'). که introduces the adjectival/relative clause describing the required companion: زندست is the archaic contraction of 'زنده است' = 'is alive', followed by مرد 'man/valiant man' as the noun being described.
segments.s287-g126: نه آنگه is a negative turn 'not so / nor then'; this is followed by a rhetorical question 'کجا زو برآرند گرد' = 'where would they bring him from?'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (contracting with a following pronoun into 'زو' in the running text), برآرند is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they would/they might bring/obtain', and گرد qualifies the bringing as 'around' or 'about'. The clause expresses impossibility: where could they possibly fetch him?
segments.s287-g127: چو = 'when'. گستهم is a proper name (Gostaham) here; the listed lemma senses do not reflect its use as a name, so its sense index is -1. شد + کشته forms a passive/result: 'was killed' (شد acting as auxiliary with the past participle کشته). در کارزار is the locative 'in battle'.
segments.s287-g128: سرآمد بر او روز is a fixed poetic turn: سرآمد 'the end/coming to a close' + بر او روز 'the day for him' = 'his day came to an end' (i.e., he died or his time was over). و links the next clause; برگشت کار = 'the affair's turning back/reversal' (برگشت as 'reversal' rather than simple 'return'), meaning the fortunes or outcome changed.
segments.s287-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause; بشنید is a third-person past 'he heard'. گودرز is the subject (proper name) and گفت is the reporting verb 'said' that introduces what follows; آن دو مرد begins the reported clause and therefore is left unfinished here, to be completed by the next segment.
segments.s287-g34: بود is the past copula 'was/were' linking the preceding topicalized noun phrase (آن دو مرد) to the predicate; گرد here forms part of a personal/epithetic name (Gord) rather than the common senses listed, لهاک and فرشیدورد are proper names joined by و 'and', so the whole means 'they were Gord-Lahak and Farshidvard.'
segments.s287-g35: برفتند is past plural 'they went/advanced'; با introduces a circumstantial phrase of manner. گردن افراختن is a verbal-noun phrase literally 'raising (their) necks' used adverbially ('with their necks raised' = proudly), where افراختن is an infinitive/verbal noun built on the verb 'to raise.'
segments.s287-g36: شکسته نشدشان is a negated passive/resultative construction: شکسته (broken) + نشد (did not become) with the clitic شان 'to them/their' attached to نشد; دل is the affected noun 'heart(s)' (possessed by شان), and از تاختن gives the cause ('from/by charging'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: literal order is دلشان از تاختن شکسته نشد.
segments.s287-g37: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایشان is the subject 'they'; از اینجا به توران is a directional phrase 'from here to Turan'; شوند is the present/subjunctive (classical) form 'they should become/go/arrive' marking the potential/conditional event — together giving 'if they go from here to Turan...'.
segments.s287-g38: آید is the verb 'comes' placed before its logical object for emphasis; بر این لشکر 'upon this army' is the prepositional subject of the misfortune, همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily', and گزند is the noun 'harm' or 'hurt' that will come — literally 'upon this army will come, verily, harm.'
segments.s287-g39: هم اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'also/at the same time'; گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' (sense list given does not include 'said'); با plus the following noun (سرکشان) marks the addressees ('to/with the ...'), so the whole means 'at that time he said to the commanders/chiefs.'
segments.s287-g40: که‌ای here functions as a vocative introductory particle 'O' (classical poetic address); نامداران دشمن‌کشان is a compound epithet 'renowned (نامداران) slayers of enemies (دشمن‌کشان)' used in direct address.
segments.s287-g129: This clause is a rhetorical question. کجا (w0) opens the question 'where/what (use)'; سود دارد (w1+w2) is a present-tense verbal phrase 'has benefit' (سود as noun, دارد as 'has'); مر او را (w3+w4+w5) is an old/poetic sequence: او را is the definite object/topic marker for 'him', while مر is an enclitic/archaic particle whose exact role is unclear here (emphatic/indirect object) and doesn't follow modern colloquial order; سپاه (w6) is the noun 'army' completing the object of سود دارد.
segments.s287-g130: کنون (w0) is 'now' setting time. دارگر داشت (w1+w2) is a past-tense clause 'if he had a دارگر' (here دارگر is listed as 'weaver' in the lexicon but may be an agentive/occupational word in context); خواهی نگاه (w3+w4) is an elliptical imperative/optative: خواهی 'if you wish' or 'do you want', نگاه is a nominal/imperative sense 'look' or 'see' — together 'if you want, look'.
segments.s287-g131: بفرمای (w0) is an imperative/request verb 'command/please do' (classical polite form). تا (w1) introduces a purposive/subordinate clause 'so that / in order that'. من (w2) is the subject 'I'. ز (w3) is the poetic preposition 'from / out of' indicating motive, and تیمار اوی (w4+w5) 'his care/for his welfare' expresses the reason.
segments.s287-g132: ببندم (w0) is a 1st-singular present/future subjunctive/imperative-like form 'that I bind / I will bind' (here 'gird myself'); کمر تنگ (w1+w2) is a compound 'tighten the waist / gird tightly' where کمر can mean 'waist' or 'girdle' and تنگ 'tight'; بر کار اوی (w3+w4+w5) 'for his business/cause' uses بر as a preposition 'for/on' and اوی as the possessive 'his'. The whole clause expresses intent to take action on his behalf.
segments.s287-g133: ور (w0) is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک (w1) is a colloquial/poetic particle meaning 'in that manner / thus'; گویی (w2) here introduces quoted speech or reported words 'you say / you would say' (its dictionary senses listed are nominal but the function is verbal/quotative in context); مرو (w3) is the quoted imperative 'do not go'; من (w4) is subject 'I'; سرم (w5) is 'my head' (here used as the object of the following verb) — note the provided lexical senses don't list 'head' so this token is irregular relative to the senses given.
segments.s287-g134: ببرم (w0) is a 1st-singular aorist/future-like verb 'I will cut/take off' (here 'I will behead / I will cut off'). The sequence ب + دین (w1+w2) is actually the prepositional/adverbial بِـ + -دین forming بدین 'with this / by this' (the lexicon entries are given separately), آبگون (w3) is an adjective 'bluish / pale' modifying خنجرم (w4) 'my dagger' (خنجر with possessive suffix). The clause describes the means of the violent act.
segments.s287-g135: که (w0) introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'that / for'. من (w1) 'I' is subject. زندگانی (w2) is 'life / living'. پس از مرگ اوی (w3+w4+w5+w6) is a temporal phrase 'after his death' where پس (w3) = 'after', از (w4) = 'of/from', مرگ (w5) = 'death', and اوی (w6) = 'his'.
segments.s287-g136: نخواهم (w0) is the negative present/subjunctive 'I do not want / I will not wish'. که (w1) introduces the content clause 'that...'. باشد (w2) is a present/subjunctive form of 'to be' meaning 'there be' or 'exist' (here 'that there be an excuse'). بهانه (w3) is 'excuse/pretext'. مجوی (w4) is a prohibitive imperative/optative 'do not seek'. Together the clause forbids searching for any excuse, because the speaker will not want to live after the other's death.
segments.s287-g57: بدو is the enclitic prepositional pronoun meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; the clause-order is verb‑final in classical style so بدو گفت = 'said to him'. نیک‌اختری is a proper name/epithet (here the speaker). تو follows the name as the addressee 'you'. ز is the archaic poetic form of از 'from/of' and links to هور (a source or quality).
segments.s287-g58: که introduces the following content clause ('that / for'), here giving a description. شیری is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'lion‑like' (leonine). و is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and'. بدخواه تو is a noun + possessive 'your ill‑wisher' (بدخواه followed by تو). همچو is a simile marker 'like/as', and گور here is the animal 'onager' (classical poetic usage).
segments.s287-g59: برو is an imperative/hortative 'go' in direct address. کآفریننده here is the fused form of که + آفریننده and functions optatively ('may the Creator...'); یار تو is 'your companion/ally' with تو as possessive. باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical wish formula).
segments.s287-g60: چو here means 'like/as' introducing the comparison. لهاک is a name or quality (given senses render it as 'eagerness' but the clause reads as a conventional simile or reference to a figure Lahāk). سیصد شکار is a quantity + noun 'three hundred captures/prey' (شکار as quarry/prey). تو باد repeats the optative 'may it be yours' (تو = 'your', باد = 'may it be').
segments.s287-g61: بپوشید is the past (or perfective) verbal form 'put on / donned' (classical prefixed بـ). گستهم is the proper name Gostaham (though the supplied sense list does not show the name); درع نبرد is a genitive/attributive construction 'the armor of battle' (درع = armor, نبرد = battle), object of the verb.
segments.s287-g62: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' linking to گردان 'troop, ranks'. که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'whom he saw' — را is the definite/object marker attached to the object of دید (past 'saw'); پدرود کرد is a light‑verb construction 'made/gave a greeting' (پدرود 'salutation', کرد 'did/made').
segments.s287-g63: This clause contains a classical idiom: برون‌رفت 'went out/sallied forth' (verbal), followed by a prepositional/ablative phrase 'از لشکر خویش' ('from his army'). Here the lexemes are split: لشکر خویش = 'his army/host' (خویش formed from خوی + ش). The token تفت appears as a name or adverbial qualifier in the line; the supplied senses do not match this usage, so the token functions as a proper name or specific term in context.
segments.s287-g64: به جنگ introduces the goal 'to battle/into battle'. دو ترک is the direct object 'two Turks'. سرافراز is an adjectival epithet 'proud/triumphant/lofty' modifying the Turks or describing the action; رفت is the past tense 'went' (narrative past). The clause is straightforward S/Adj + verb word order typical of epic narrative.
segments.s287-g201: This is an oath introduced by the preposition به used in the sense 'by' (a formulaic swearing). جان و سر is a twofold accusative-like phrase 'life and head' (both objects of به). شاه روشن‌روان places the epithet روشن‌روان after شاه (epithet following noun, typical Persian poetic order): 'the bright‑souled king.'
segments.s287-g202: A second oath parallel to the first: به جان = 'by the life (of)'. نیا here functions as a name/forebear term attached to the following epithets; نامور پهلوان is an epithet phrase 'renowned champion' with the adjective نامور before the noun پهلوان (epithet order is flexible in poetry).
segments.s287-g203: به introduces purpose or motive here: 'for / in revenge of.' کین سیاوش = 'the vengeance of Siyâvash' (کین = vengeance). کز is the relative/prepositional contraction of که + از meaning 'from/of which' and links to این رزمگاه: 'from this battlefield.'
segments.s287-g204: تو برگردی is a jussive/subjunctive-like clause 'that you turn back / may turn back' (purpose/result clause). و links the two coordinated clauses. من بپویم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I go/set out' (ب‑ + پویم) meaning 'I will go on / pursue the road.' به راه = 'toward/on the road.'
segments.s287-g205: نخواهم ... کرد here forms a negative intentional/future sense: 'I will not (do)...' with کرد as the main verb 'to do/make.' بر این کار = 'about/on this matter' (بر marking the domain), and فرمانت is 'your command/order' (فرمان with the 2nd‑person enclitic -ت). Together: 'I will not issue you a command in this matter.'
segments.s287-g206: که introduces a content clause 'that / as if'. گویی here functions idiomatically 'as if (you were) saying...' (an expression of reported or imagined speech). مرا ... را is the object 'me' (مرا split into clitic elements here) with را as the definite/object marker; بازگرد is the verbal root 'turn back / send back' used as an imperative/infinitival sense, and از نبرد 'from the battle' marks origin.
segments.s287-g207: چو = 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'heard'. Note the verb precedes the subject (بشنید گیو = 'Giv heard' / 'When Giv heard'); این سخن = 'these words / this speech'. بازگشت here is verbal past 'he returned / turned back.'
segments.s287-g208: بر او آفرین کرد = 'he praised him' (بر marking the object/target of the praise, آفرین as the exclamation/noun 'praise' and کرد as the light verb 'did/made'). و اندر گذشت = 'and passed on / went away' with اندر 'in/into' and گذشت the past 'passed / went on.'
segments.s287-g1: چو is the classical poetic conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. لشکر is the subject 'the army'. چنین modifies پاسخ ('such an answer'), and آراستند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they fashioned/made' placed after its object phrase (typical SOV order in Persian poetry).
segments.s287-g2: دو پرمایه is a poetic noun phrase 'two (mighty/substantial) ones' serving as subject. از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from their place(s)', and برخاستند is the past‑plural verb 'they rose up/rose from (their) places', with the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s287-g3: بدانست is a past tense verb 'knew/realized' placed before the following compound subject (a common poetic word order: verb first for emphasis). لهاک and فرشیدورد are the names/agents who are understood as the ones perceiving; the verb remains in the past form.
segments.s287-g4: کشان is a masdar/gerundial form meaning 'dragging/delay' used predicatively. نیست is the negative copula 'is not'. هنگام introduces the temporal complement 'at the time of', followed by the coordinated nouns ننگ و نبرد 'shame and battle' specifying which time is meant. The whole phrase reads 'it is not the time for dragging (delay): (it is) the time of shame and fight.'
segments.s287-g5: همی is an adverbial particle often marking habitual or emphatic assertion ('commonly/indeed/ever'), here preceding the predicate. راست modifies گویند to mean 'speak truth', but note گویند is placed in a poetic present/habitual form and is followed by its subject 'لشکر همه' (the entire host), a verb‑first word order used for emphasis.
segments.s287-g6: تبه گردَد is a standard verbal phrase 'will become ruined/come to ruin' (تبه used as 'ruin' or 'to be ruined' with the inchoative گردد). از introduces the cause 'because of/from', بی‑شبانی is a compound meaning 'lack of night‑watch' (failure to post guards at night), and رمه 'the flock/herd' names the agent whose neglect causes the ruin; the whole phrase gives the reason for the host's ruin.
segments.s287-g7: به پدرود کردند is a light prepositional phrase plus verb: به + پدرود ('with a farewell') and کردند (past plural 'they did/said'), i.e., 'they bade farewell'. گــرفتند ساز inverts the normal object‑verb order (poetic inversion): گرفتند is past plural 'they took/ seized' and ساز 'instrument' is the object, so 'they took up (their) instruments.'
segments.s287-g8: بیابان گرفتند is another verb phrase with object before verb by poetic license: بیابان 'the wilderness/desert' and گرفتند 'they took' together mean 'they took to the wilderness'. و connects the final noun phrase راه دراز 'the long road' (راه = road/path modified by the adjective دراز).
segments.s287-g97: چو + past verb is the common classical formula for 'when (he) heard'. Here چو (a subordinating particle) + بشنید (past, 3rd‑person) gives 'when he heard'; گودرز is the subject placed after the verb (typical flexible Classical Persian order). گفتار اوی is a possessive noun phrase 'his speech' (گفتار = 'speech'; اوی = possessive 'his').
segments.s287-g98: کشیدن here is an infinitival/gerundial use meaning 'to carry out / set about' (a common poetic use). بدان = به + آن ('to/with that' or 'about that') links the action to the previously mentioned matter. کار تیمار اوی is the object phrase 'the affair / task of care of his' i.e. 'that task, with his care' so the whole clause says he proceeded to (or took pains over) that business.
segments.s287-g99: پس introduces a following action ('then'). اندیشه is a nominal root meaning 'thought' and with کرد (past of کردن) forms the light‑verb construction اندیشه کرد = 'he thought / he considered'. اندر آن یک زمان is a temporal phrase 'for that one moment / during that one time' (اندر = 'in', آن یک زمان = 'that one time').
segments.s287-g100: هم از بد introduces an additional cause/possibility ('also because of misfortune/ill'), followed by a relative clause introduced by که. می‌برد is imperfective/present stem 'was carrying / was causing / was leading to' (here 'led to' or 'gave rise to'), with بیژن as its object/possessed person and گمان 'suspicion' as the resultant state: 'that (this) caused Bijan's suspicion.'
segments.s287-g101: به گردان چنین گفت is the speech frame 'said thus to the corps/troop' (به + گردان = 'to the troop'; چنین = 'thus'; گفت here marks the act of speaking). سالار شاه follows as the speaker's title/address: سالار = 'commander' and شاه = 'king' (here either as vocative or as appositive identifying the addressee/authority).
segments.s287-g102: This is the beginning of the reported speech introduced by که ('that'). هر کس که جوید همی نام و گاه is a generic relative clause: هر کس که = 'whoever', جوید (present/stative stem) = 'seeks' (a habitual present), همی adds continuative/emphatic sense ('always / indeed'), and نام و گاه = 'name and place/status' (i.e. fame and position). The clause sets up a proverb-like statement about those who seek fame and rank.
segments.s287-g103: پس آغاز = 'then' (پس). گستهم is an archaic/poetic 1st‑person perfect/preterite 'I wandered' (a fused form). رفت is the simple past 'went'; together they narrate motion 'I went off, I wandered'. باید expresses necessity ('it is necessary / one must'), and دمان here is a temporal/locative notion (classical word used for a short time/holding position) so باید دمان yields 'one must remain/keep watch for a while' (idiomatic classical usage).
segments.s287-g104: مر = 'to me / for me' (dative/emphatic), او = 'him' and را is the definite object/ topicalizing marker attaching to او. بدن یار is a compact phrase here meaning 'without a companion / bereft of an ally' (بدن in this idiom functions to create the 'without' sense in older usage), and با بدگمان = 'with ill‑feeling / suspiciously' (با + adjective). Grammatically, the clause states the speaker's intention regarding that person: to leave/keep him alone and mistrustful.
segments.s287-g73: همه (subject) + بازگشتند (past plural verb) = 'all returned/turned back.' یکسر is an adverb meaning 'at once'/'entirely' modifying the action; ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' giving origin. Word order is poetic (verb before adverbial phrase), but grammatically it's Subject — Verb — (Adverb) — Prepositional phrase.
segments.s287-g74: خروشان is a participial/adverbial word describing the manner ('tumultuous/roaring') of the following verb. برفتند is the past plural 'they went/advanced' (بر‑ is an archaic/poetic prefix on رفتند indicating movement). نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near/close to the king' that follows the verb as its directional/locative complement.
segments.s287-g75: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' بشنید is past simple 'he heard' (third person). بیژن is the subject (Bijan). که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'; گستهم here functions as the name/subject of that subordinate clause (the form is ambiguous in the supplied senses), and رفت is the past verb 'went/left' referring to that action in the subordinate clause.
segments.s287-g76: ز لشکر is an origin phrase 'from the army.' به آورد is a causative/periphrastic verbal phrase here: آورد used with به to mean 'brought/caused to come upon (him).' لهاک تفت is a nominal compound where لهاک 'eagerness/haste' is modified by تفت 'burning/heat' — together 'a burning eagerness' that is the thing brought upon him.
segments.s287-g77: گمانی چنان = 'a thought so/such a thought' (گمانی: a supposition/idea; چنان: 'so'/'such'). برد here is used as a verb 'took/seized' (archaic/poetic use of بردن) meaning 'was seized by' rather than the noun senses listed; بیژن is the subject receiving that feeling. که introduces the following clause about what he thought/worry was.
segments.s287-g78: چو = 'when.' تنگ اندر آید is a temporal clause 'when he comes/enters into the narrow (place)' (اندر = 'in/into', آید = present/episodic 'comes' used for a future/expected event). به دشت دغو = 'to/into the plain (will be) clamor' — دغو 'uproar/clamour' is the resulting state; the verb is implied or elliptic as common in poetry.
segments.s287-g79: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal 'it must not be that' / 'they must not.' The following nouns لهاک و فرشیدورد are the agents (Lahak and Farshidvard). Note: لهاک here is a proper name (not the lexical sense 'eagerness') and فرشیدورد is a personal name.
segments.s287-g80: برآرند is a present-subjunctive/jussive third‑person plural verb 'they raise/bring forth' (used after نباید که to express prohibition). از او = 'from him'; خاک روز نبرد is a genitive construction 'the dust of the day of battle' (خاک = dust; روز = day; نبرد = battle) — idiomatically 'raise the dust of battle' = provoke fighting. Word order is compact and poetic but parses as: 'نباید که [they] برآرند [از او] [خاکِ روزِ نبرد]'.
segments.s287-g161: به هر کار is a prepositional phrase placed up front meaning 'in/for every matter'; مجوی is a prohibitive/negative imperative (classical/poetic) from جستن, meaning 'do not seek/ask'. درد دلم را is the definite object: درد = 'pain/sorrow', دل+م is 'my heart', and را marks the whole phrase as the definite object of مجوی.
segments.s287-g162: به پیران is a prepositional phrase 'to the elders' (پیران = elders). سر از من here uses سر in the sense 'matter/secret/concerning' (i.e. 'of me/about me') with از introducing the content. چه باید + بگوی is a modal construction: باید 'ought/must' followed by the subjunctive/infinitival form بگوی (classical poetic shape) giving 'what ought (I/one) to say'. The subject is omitted (first person implied by context).
segments.s287-g163: جز از تو is the idiom 'except for you' (جز ... از). به گیتیم is 'in my world' — گیتیم is گیتی + possessive -م 'my world' with the locative/locutional preposition به. فرزند نیست is an existential negation: 'there is no child' (فرزند + نیست). Word order places the locative phrase before the existential predicate.
segments.s287-g164: روانم is 'my soul/spirit' (روان + possessive -م). به درد تو uses به + noun to indicate 'because of/at/with regard to your pain' (درد تو = your suffering). خرسند نیست is a predicative adjective + negative copula: 'is not pleased/content'. Overall: 'My soul is not content because of your pain.'
segments.s287-g165: بدی ده combines a noun (بدی 'evil/harm') followed by ده 'ten' — here best read as a temporal/quantifying phrase 'ten nights and days' together with شبان روز (a poetic/archaic pairing meaning 'nights and days'). بر پشت زین is the locative phrase 'on the saddle's back' (بر + پشت + زین). Note شبان is an archaic/poetic pluralizing or collocation for 'nights'.
segments.s287-g166: کشیده is a past participle / adjectival form 'drawn/dragged' or 'having been drawn'; به بدخواه indicates direction/agental relation 'toward/by the ill-wisher (enemy)'; بر تیغ کین is a prepositional phrase 'onto/upon the sword of vengeance' (تیغ = sword, کین = vengeance). The whole phrase describes being dragged by a foe toward the avenging sword.
segments.s287-g167: بسودی is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'you will profit/it will avail' (i.e. 'you may gain/benefit'). به خفتان is 'among sleepers' (خفتان = those who sleep; poetic plural), and و خود اندرون = 'and yourself inside/secure within' (خود reflexive/emphatic, اندرون = inside). The clause sets up a supposed safe/beneficial position.
segments.s287-g168: نخواهی here is the negative modal 'you will not/will not wish'; همی is a classical adverb adding continuative/emphatic force ('thus/still'); سیر گشتن is the compound 'to become sated' (سیر = satiety, گشتن = to become); ز خون = 'from/by blood' (poetic ز = از). Together: 'you will not become sated by bloodshed.'
segments.s287-g105: ندادند is past-tense negative, 3rd-person plural ('they did not give'); Persian often uses a plural verb impersonally to mean 'no one gave'. پاسخ کس is the object ('an answer / anyone to answer') and از انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the assembly' (word order: verb - object - source).
segments.s287-g106: نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor'. غمخواره here is a noun meaning 'a grieving/sympathizing person'; بد کس literally 'bad person' = 'callous/ill-disposed person'; آسوده‌تن is a compound noun 'one at ease / carefree person'. The whole phrase lists the kinds of people who did not answer.
segments.s287-g107: به گودرز 'to Goudarz' (prepositional phrase) comes before the reporting verb. پس گفت بیژن 'then Bijan said' — پس 'then/afterwards'; گفت is the past-tense reporting verb. که introduces the reported content ('that').
segments.s287-g108: جز از من 'except from me' (i.e. 'but me') + نباشدش is a negative jussive/subjunctive form meaning 'there is not / there would be none' with the enclitic ش = 'for him/her/it'; together meaning 'there is no one except me for him'. فریادرس is a substantive 'rescuer/succorer' and completes the thought.
segments.s287-g109: که آید 'who would come' (که = relative/that; آید present/subjunctive/optative form here meaning 'would/should come'). ز گردان 'from the ranks/troop' (ز = from). بدین کار پیش is 'forward for this task' — بدین is a contraction meaning 'to/for this' and کار = 'task'; پیش here has the sense 'forward/in front'.
segments.s287-g110: به سیری نیامد کس is an idiomatic negative statement: نیامد (did not come / will not come) with کس ('anyone' effectively 'no one' because of the negation). به سیری literally 'to satisfaction' — here idiomatically 'willingly' or 'out of contentment'; از جان خویش 'from his life' (i.e. 'sacrifice his life'). The clause says no one would willingly give up his life for this.
segments.s287-g111: مرا رفت باید is an inversion common in older Persian: 'مرا' (object/emphatic 'me' with را), رفت (past infinitive/verb 'to go') and باید ('it is necessary') combine to mean 'I must go' or more literally 'for me it is necessary to go'. که از کار اوی: که introduces the reason ('because'); از کار اوی = 'because of his affair / his plight' (اوی = 'his').
segments.s287-g112: دلم پر ز درد است 'my heart is full of pain': دل + possessive enclitic (م) = 'my heart'; پر ز = 'full of'; درد = emotional pain/sorrow; است = 'is'. و پر آب روی 'and (my) face full of water' — water here is literal but idiomatic for tears, so 'face full of tears'. Word order is subject (possessed body-part) → predicate adjective phrase.
segments.s287-g25: پس (then) + از + دیده گه forms a prepositional temporal/spatial phrase 'then/from the watch-post'. دیده‌بان (the sentinel/lookout) is the subject and کرد غو is a light-verb construction: کرد (to do) + غو (uproar) = 'raised an outcry/caused a tumult.' Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase first, then subject, then the periphrastic verb phrase.
segments.s287-g26: که‌ای here is the archaic vocative (ὠ / 'که ای' = 'O') calling out to the addressees. سرفرازان is a plural vocative 'proud/victorious ones' and و گردان نو links the second addressee 'and the fresh/new troop' (گردان = detachment). Adjective نو follows the noun in the usual Persian order for emphasis.
segments.s287-g27: از این لشکر ترک is a prepositional phrase 'from this army of Turks.' The numeral phrase دو نامدار ('two famed [men]') follows as the internal argument; classical Persian often places numerals before the noun they count. The implied noun after نامدار is 'men/warriors.'
segments.s287-g28: برون‌رفت here conveys the action 'issued forth / rode out' though the supplied dictionary senses are nominal; in classical usage this form functions as a past action ('they went out'). با نامور ده سوار is an instrumental/companional phrase 'with ten renowned horsemen' (با + [adjective + numeral + noun]), where نامور modifies سوار and ده is the numeral before the noun.
segments.s287-g29: چنان … که is a correlative pair meaning 'so … that.' چنان (so) sets up the degree, با طلایه ('upon/against the vanguard') is the prepositional/target phrase, and برآویختند is the past-tense, third-person plural verb 'they sprang upon/attacked.' Word order places the manner/target before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s287-g30: که introduces the result clause 'so that.' با خاک is an instrumental/comitative prepositional phrase 'with earth/dust,' خون را is the definite object (را marks خون as the object), and برآمیختند is the past plural 'they mixed/mingled.' The clause therefore reads literally 'that with earth they mixed the blood.'
segments.s287-g31: تنی هشت is an object phrase 'eight bodies/barely eight men' (تنی = bodily / a certain number of persons) followed by the verb کشتند 'they killed' (past third-person plural). ایرانیان 'the Iranians' appears after the verb as the subject (postverbal subject for emphasis). The lexical sense for کشتند (to kill) is not among the provided senses, so it is marked -1.
segments.s287-g32: دو تن ('two persons') is the counted noun phrase; تیز is an adjectival quality 'swift' modifying them, and رفتند is the past plural 'they went' used here as 'they dashed/advanced.' بسته میان is a short phrase where بسته (bound/tied/closely joined) describes their mutual posture and میان 'among/in the midst' gives the location—together: 'bound/tightly together amid (the ranks).' Word order places descriptive adjectives and participles after the noun cluster.
segments.s287-g65: همی + گفت is a classical/perfective habitual construction: همی here gives an iterative/continuous sense and گفت is the past 'said' — together 'kept saying'. لشکر is the subject ('the army'). همه سر به سر is an adverbial phrase meaning 'all together' or 'one and all' (literally 'head to head'), modifying how they spoke.
segments.s287-g66: که introduces a reported subordinate clause 'that...'. گستهم را is the affected person/thing marked by the object/topic marker را (here indicating who is to be afflicted). ز این بد = 'from/because of this (bad thing)'; آید is present/past-use verb 'comes' or 'happens' (used with future/looming sense), and به سر is an idiom 'to the head' meaning 'to befall' someone. Together: 'that this bad thing will come upon گستهم.'
segments.s287-g67: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' modifying لشکر 'a contingent/one army'. از نزد is a compound prepositional phrase 'from the presence/side of' (نزد being 'in the presence of' or 'with'), so از نزد افراسیاب = 'from Afrasiab's side / from Afrasiab's camp'.
segments.s287-g68: همی رفت again gives an iterative/habitual past sense 'kept going / went'. بر سان = 'in the manner of / like' (سان = 'like'), so بر سان کشتی بر آب is a simile 'like a ship upon the water' with the second بر meaning 'on/upon'. Word order mirrors classical Persian simile construction.
segments.s287-g69: به یاری is a prepositional phrase 'to the aid / for help'. همه جنگجو = 'all the warriors' (همه modifying جنگجو). آمدند is past plural 'they came' — here narrative 'they came to give help', the verb agrees with the plural subject 'جنگجو'.
segments.s287-g70: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when / as'. نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near / approaching'. دشت دغو is a noun phrase 'the plain of clamor/uproar' (دغو qualifying دشت). آمدند again is 'they came' (referring to the same fighters) — the clause says that as they neared the plain of uproar, they arrived / tumult was present.
segments.s287-g71: خبر شد بدیشان is an idiom 'news became known to them / they received word' (شد here = 'happened/became' in the sense 'it became known'). که introduces the content of the news: پیران گذشت — 'Piran passed' (گذشت is simple past 'passed', contextually ambiguous between 'passed by' or 'passed away').
segments.s287-g72: نبرد دلیران is a genitive construction 'the battle of the brave (men)'. دگرگونه is an adjective/adverb 'different; changed', and گشت is past of گشتن 'became / turned' — together 'the brave men's battle turned different' meaning the course of the battle changed.
segments.s287-g9: درفشی (a standard-bearer) is the noun being described by the following participial phrase. گرفته is a past participle / adjectival verb meaning 'having taken' or 'holding' (here 'held'); the phrase گرفته به دست literally 'held in (the) hand' uses به + دست to mark the manner/place ('in the hand'). اندرون ('within, inside') is an adverbial/locative that localizes the action (i.e. held inward/among the ranks). The whole segment is an attributive phrase describing someone who is holding the banner in hand.
segments.s287-g10: پر از is the construction 'full of' (پر = 'full', followed by the preposition از = 'of'); درد دل is a compound meaning 'pain of the heart' or 'heartache' (درد qualified by دل). دیدگان پر ز خون is a parallel clause: دیدگان 'the eyes' + پر 'full' + ز (poetic از) 'of' + خون 'blood', i.e. 'eyes full of blood'. The two halves are coordinated descriptions of the same person.
segments.s287-g11: برفتند is simple past, 3rd-person-plural 'they went/rode off.' با introduces the company 'with'; نامور 'renowned' modifies the following noun; ده سوار is a numeral + noun 'ten horsemen.' The verb precedes the accompanying prepositional phrase, a common word order in poetic Persian.
segments.s287-g12: دلیران is a plural noun/adjective 'the brave ones' used appositionally to describe those horsemen; و 'and' links a second descriptor; شایسته کارزار is a nominal phrase 'worthy/fit for battle' where شایسته modifies کارزار ('battle'). Together these words further characterize the ten horsemen.
segments.s287-g13: به ره بر ز ایران is a locative phrase 'on the road from Iran' (به ره = 'on/to the road'; بر here reinforces location 'on'; ز ایران = 'from Iran'). سواران بدند: سواران 'the riders/horsemen' + بدند (archaic past/plural of بودن) 'they were' — the clause states that on that road there were mounted horsemen. The verb بدند is an older Persian form equivalent to بودند/هستند in context.
segments.s287-g14: نگهبان 'guard' is the predicate noun; آن نامداران 'those famed/illustrious ones' is the genitive/demonstrative phrase being guarded; بدند again is 'they were.' The full clause means 'they were the guards of those renowned men,' with the subject understood from the previous clause (the riders).
segments.s287-g15: برانگیختند is a past/plural verb 'they aroused/roused' or here 'sprang up/rose' (used with animals); اسب ترکان is a genitive sequence 'the horses of the Turks.' ز جای 'from their places' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place'). Together: 'the Turks' horses sprang up from their places.'
segments.s287-g16: طلایه 'the vanguard/forefront' is the subject; بیفشارد is an older-form verb meaning 'pressed/pressed on' or 'thrust/advanced' (here 'pressed forward'); با جای پای literally 'with the place of the foot' idiomatically means 'with the trampling/stamping of their feet' (با = 'with', جای پای = 'footfall/footprint'). The clause describes the vanguard advancing by the force of their steps.
segments.s287-g17: یکی here is the indefinite 'one / a certain', introducing a single event. ناسگالیده‌شان is the adjective ناسگالیده 'ill‑fated' with the 3rd‑person plural possessive enclitic ‑شان ('their' / 'of theirs'), used elliptically to qualify the forthcoming noun. جنگ is the head noun 'battle / combat' followed by the past verb خاست (archaic poetic past of خاستن) meaning 'arose / began' — Persian VSO order (verb after subject/adverbial) is common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s287-g18: که introduces a result/relative clause ('such that' / 'so that'). از خون زمین is a prepositional phrase 'from / by blood the earth' (از = 'from / because of', خون = 'blood', زمین = 'earth/ground'). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became / turned (into)' (3rd sg.). با + noun + صفت (با کوه راست) is a poetic simile/qualifier: با = 'with / as', کوه = 'mountain', راست = 'straight/upright' (here 'towering/straight like a mountain'); the clause therefore says the earth, by blood, became (as) a mountain in appearance — word order is typical of classical Persian, with the verb following its complements.
segments.s287-g19: بکشتند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural of کشتن 'they killed' (verb first, as is frequent in narrative Persian). ایرانیان is the agent/subject 'the Iranians'. هشت مرد is the direct object 'eight men / eight warriors' (مرد here denotes men/warriors — a heroic sense). The sequence shows VSO order and a numeric quantifier directly before the noun.
segments.s287-g20: دلیران و شیران are post‑nominal epithets describing those eight men: دلیران 'the brave ones' and شیران 'the lions' (metaphorical for brave men). روز نبرد is a genitive/time phrase 'the day of battle' (روز = 'day', نبرد = 'battle'); in poetry the ezāfe linking these elements is often omitted, so شیرانِ روزِ نبرد = 'lions of the battle‑day'. The whole segment is appositional, emphasizing their valor.
segments.s287-g21: وز here is an archaic/poetic preposition equivalent to 'from' or a discourse marker meaning 'then / from', a form not common in modern colloquial Persian (the supplied senses did not include 'from', so this is an archaic usage). آنجا = 'there'; برفتند is past, 3rd‑person plural of رفتن 'they went / departed'. هر دو is the idiom 'both' (هر = each → here 'both'), and دلیر is the adjective 'brave' modifying those two. Word order: prepositional/time phrase → verb → subject phrase is typical of classical narrative.
segments.s287-g22: به راه بیابان is a directional prepositional phrase: به = 'toward / on / to', راه = 'road / way', بیابان = 'desert / wilderness' → 'to/on the desert road'. به کردار شیر is an adverbial simile 'in the manner (کردار) of a lion (شیر)'; the second به functions adverbially ('‑ly / in the manner of'), and classical Persian often omits the ezāfe between کردار and شیر in verse. The phrase as a whole describes their conduct.
segments.s287-g23: ز ترکان = 'from the Turks' (ز is the poetic variant of از). جز این دو سرافراز = 'except these two triumphant/proud ones' (جز introduces the exception). گرد here is a short poetic particle/terminating word used in older Persian to mark a group or add emphasis; it is a poetic flourish rather than carrying a concrete modern meaning (its precise function is archaic). The whole segment sets up the exception: only these two remained/are exempt.
segments.s287-g24: ز دست طلایه = 'by the hand / at the hand of the vanguard' (ز = 'by/from', دست = 'hand/power', طلایه = 'vanguard' — ezāfe linking is poetically compressed). دگر جان نبرد = 'no other life was taken' — دگر 'other' modifies جان 'life / soul', and نبرد here functions as the past verb 'took' (classical/poetic use of بردن in past narrative). Together the clause says that, aside from the two named, the vanguard did not take another life.
segments.s287-g89: This phrase names the two figures: دو تن is the numeral + classifier 'two persons'; نامداران is a post-nominal attributive meaning 'renowned ones'; توران سپاه is a poetic inversion (normally سپاهِ توران) meaning 'the army/host of Turan'. The whole chunk = 'two renowned persons of Turan's army.'
segments.s287-g90: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed'. ز این‌سان functions adverbially ('thus / in this manner') and modifies the verb. دلاور here describes the subjects as 'valiant' (adjectival use). به راه is the locative phrase 'on the road / on their way' with به + noun.
segments.s287-g91: ز at the start works as the comparative 'than' (poetic variant of از). هومان و پیران names the comparatives (here proper names are used). دلاورترند is the comparative adjective + plural copula ('are more valiant' or 'are braver').
segments.s287-g92: This is a predicative phrase: به گوهر = 'as a jewel / like a gem', بزرگان آن = 'of the great ones of that (land)'; کشورند is the verb-like ending indicating 'they belong to / are of that country' — together: 'they are the jewel of the great men of that country.'
segments.s287-g93: کنون = 'now'. گستهم is a first‑person form meaning 'I was distraught/worried' (an older/poetic verb form) and is followed by شد 'became' to mark the change of state. به جنگ دو تن = 'by/at the battle of the two' (به + noun gives the cause/scene).
segments.s287-g94: نباید is an impersonal prohibition 'it must not / should not'. که introduces the subordinate clause ('that ...'). آید is the verb 'comes' (here expressing possibility/occurrence). بر او is the prepositional phrase 'upon him', and بر شکن is a nominal predicate 'breaker/one who would break/attack' — so the clause says 'it must not be that a breaker comes upon him.'
segments.s287-g95: همه کام ما = 'all our desires/hopes' (subject). بازگردد is a present/subjunctive or gnomic third‑person verb 'turn back / revert'; به درد is the resultative/prepositional phrase 'to pain / to sorrow' — together: 'all our hopes turn to sorrow.'
segments.s287-g96: چو = 'when'. کم گردد is the verb phrase 'becomes few / is diminished' (کم + گردد, گردد as intransitive 'become'). از لشکر آن رادمرد = 'of the army/host of that nobleman' (از marks separation/partitive), so the temporal clause means 'when the number from that nobleman's host becomes small.'
segments.s287-g137: بدو (به + او) is a prepositional/enclitic phrase 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past of 'to say' but here appears in a poetic inventory where its normal lexical sense ('said') is not listed in the provided sense set (so treat it as the reporting verb introducing speech). گودرز is the subject (proper name) placed after گفت by poetic inversion. بشتاب is an imperative 'hurry!' and پیش is an adverb meaning 'forward / forth' modifying that imperative; together بشتاب پیش forms the quoted command.
segments.s287-g138: اگر نیست opens a conditional ('if there is not' / 'if you are not'); مهر تو is 'your affection/attachment' (مهر = love/affection, تو = you); بر جان خویش is a prepositional phrase 'upon your life' where جان = 'life/soul' and خوی + ش together function as خویش 'one's own' (خوی = root + ش = the 3rd-person/possessive element). The meaning: 'if your love is not for your own life' ≈ 'if you are not attached to your life.'
segments.s287-g139: نیابی is the negative form of یافتن in this context meaning 'you will not find' or 'you do not find' (poetic present/future sense). همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle 'ever/still' (here emphasising the negation). سیری is 'satisfaction/satiety', and از کارزار is a genitive phrase 'from the battle' / 'in battle' — together: 'You will never find satisfaction from battle.'
segments.s287-g140: This is a sequence of short imperatives/commands. کمر بند literally 'bind the waist' (کمر = waist/girdle, بند = band/binding) = 'gird your loins/fasten your belt.' و is the conjunction 'and.' ببسیچ is an archaic/obscure form (here functioning as another imperative meaning roughly 'brace/prepare' — the exact lexical sense is unclear). و again 'and.' سر بر is a head-locative construction 'head upon/against' and مخار is an uncertain noun (the object/place toward which the head is put). Overall the clause lists martial preparations: 'gird your belt, brace yourself, and put your head against (the foe/position).'
segments.s287-g141: نسوزد is a negative jussive/optative/3rd-person form of سوزیدن 'to burn' — here best rendered as a wish/negative command 'may it not burn' / 'do not let it burn.' همانا is an emphatic particle 'indeed' or 'surely.' دلت is دل + -ت 'your heart.' بر پدر is a prepositional phrase 'for/over your father' — so: 'Do not let your heart be consumed for your father.'
segments.s287-g142: که introduces the reason/explanatory clause 'for / because.' هزمان means 'simultaneously / at the same time.' مر is the clitic/pronoun 'me' and او را 'him' with را marking the definite object; the sequence indicates that the following action affects both 'him' and 'me' (i.e. will cause grief to both). بسوزی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/present of 'to burn' used here in the sense 'you will (cause to) burn' or 'you grieve.' جگر is used metaphorically as 'heart/seat of feeling' (i.e. emotional pain). The clause: 'for by grieving him you will at the same time burn my heart.'
segments.s287-g143: چو is the conjunction 'when' (temporal). بشنید is the past 3rd-person of شنیدن 'he heard/it was heard.' بیژن is the subject name placed after the verb by poetic order. فرو برد سر is a verb + object phrase 'lowered (فرو برد) the head (سر)' = 'he bowed his head down.'
segments.s287-g144: زمین را ببوسید: زمین (the ground) + را (definite object marker) + ببوسید (past tense 'he kissed') — 'he kissed the ground.' و آمد به در: و 'and', آمد 'he came', به در 'to the door / toward the doorway' — 'and he came to the door.'
segments.s287-g153: This is an adverbial-temporal clause: هم اندر زمان = 'at the same moment/also in that time' (هم = 'also/then'; اندر = archaic/prepositional 'in/within'; زمان = 'time/moment'). گیو is the subject (proper name), برجست is the past action 'sprang up/rose' (poetic past), and زود is a manner adverb 'quickly/soon' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s287-g154: نشست here means 'alighted/dismounted' (classical usage: نشست از برِ اسب = get down from the horse). از بر is the classical prepositional frame meaning 'from off (upon)' (از = 'from', بر = 'on/upon'), تازی اسبی is 'Arabian horse/steed' (تازی used adjectivally with اسبی), and چو دود is a simile 'like smoke' modifying the manner of dismounting.
segments.s287-g155: بیامد به ره = 'he came onto the road' (بیامد = simple past 'came'). The sequence بر چو او را بدید is a compressed temporal clause: چو = 'when'; او را بدید = 'he saw him' with را marking the definite object. The particle بر (here 'on/upon' or a resumptive/adverbial particle) is poetic placement before چو and ties the seeing to the action of arrival.
segments.s287-g156: به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste/quickly' (به + noun). عنانش is عنان + the 3rd-person singular enclitic -ش 'his reins'. به یکسو = 'to one side' (به = to/towards, یکسو = one side). کشید is the past 'drew/pulled' (he pulled his reins). Note the enclitic pronoun on عنان marks possession, and the verb is simple past.
segments.s287-g157: بدو گفت is the fixed phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت here functions as past 'said' although the supplied senses list is for noun; context shows verbal use). چندین زدم داستان is object-verb order typical of older Persian: چندین = 'many times/several times', زدم (from زدن) used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'I have recited/told' the tale, and داستان = 'tale/story'.
segments.s287-g158: نخواهی is the negative future/volitional 2nd-person form here (classical usage meaning 'you will not' / 'you will not be willing'); همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/still' in older Persian); بود is the infinitive/root 'to be' (here part of the copular construction 'you will not be'); همداستان is an adjective/noun 'of one mind/agreeing with me'. The clause as a whole means 'you will not, indeed, be of the same mind.'
segments.s287-g159: که introduces a subordinate/optative clause 'that/so that'; باشم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive of بودن 'may I be / that I be' (expressing desire/optative), به تو = 'to/with you' (indirect object/benefactive), شادمان = predicate adjective 'glad/happy', and یک زمان = 'for a while/for a time' (temporal adverbial). The clause expresses a wish or purpose 'that I might be happy with you for a time.'
segments.s287-g160: کجا = 'where'. رفت خواهی is an inverted poetic ordering of the verb + auxiliary: رفت (root 'go') placed before خواهی (future auxiliary 'you will'), together meaning 'where will you go?' بدین سان = 'in this way/thus' (poetic compound; here written in pieces in the word list), and دمان (دامان) is a noun 'skirt/retreat/hem' often used metaphorically; in context it asks rhetorically 'will you thus depart/retire?'. Note the inversion (verb before auxiliary) is typical in older/epic Persian.
segments.s287-g81: نشست is a past tense, 3rd-person-singular verb meaning ‘sat/settled’ (an archaic/simple past form). از بر دیزه is a prepositional phrase in old/poetic word order: از (from/off) + بر (on/upon) + دیزه (fortress) — together ‘from off the fortress’. راه‌جوی is the nominal subject/epithet ‘the way-seeker/traveler’ placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s287-g82: به نزدیک گودرز is a directional phrase: به (towards) + نزدیک (near) + گودرز (proper name). بنهاد is an archaic past-tense verb ‘placed/put’ (here ‘set/turned’), and روی (face) is its object: بنهاد روی = ‘he set/turned (his) face’. The subject (راه‌جوی) is implied from the previous segment.
segments.s287-g83: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’. چشمش is the possessed noun ‘his eye’ (چشم + ـش). به روی نیا is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘upon the face of Niyâ’ (نیا here functions as a proper/name or referent), and برفتاد is an older past tense meaning ‘fell/turned’ (his gaze fell / turned toward).
segments.s287-g84: خروشید is an intransitive past ‘he cried out / roared’. و simply links clauses. چندی is an adverb ‘for a while’. سخن کرد is the common compound ‘spoke / said’ (سخن as object/phrase + کرد, past of کردن). یاد is the object meaning ‘memory / remembrance’ — together ‘he cried out and for a while spoke, recalling/reminding’. Word order (object after کرد) is idiomatic in poetic diction.
segments.s287-g85: نه خوب آید is a fixed negative phrase: نه (not) + خوب آید (‘it comes/it is fitting’) — here meaning ‘it is not fitting/it is ill’; ای پهلوان is a vocative ‘O champion’. از خرد literally ‘from wisdom’ = ‘according to wisdom’ (i.e. ‘in terms of good judgment’). The whole phrase expresses a moral reproach.
segments.s287-g86: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ‘that / namely’. هر is the quantifier ‘every/any’. نامداری here refers to a notable or celebrated person (poetically used as ‘a famed man’). که فرمان برد is an older collocation meaning ‘who takes/carries out (a) command’ — فرمان is ‘command’ and برد is past-tense ‘bore/took’ (in context idiomatically = ‘submit to/obey’). The clause qualifies the general statement in the previous segment.
segments.s287-g87: مر is an archaic form of مرا (me/to me). او را is the direct object ‘him’ (را marks definiteness/object). به خیره بکشتن is an infinitival phrase: به خیره (an adverbial phrase, ‘openly/straightforwardly / outright’) + بکشتن (infinitive ‘to kill’). دهی is a present/jussive form of دادن ‘give’ (here a request/command: ‘give me [permission/material] to kill him’), so the structure is a double-object causative: ‘give him to me to kill.’
segments.s287-g88: بهانه is the noun ‘excuse / pretext’. به چرخ فلک is a prepositional phrase ‘to/unto the wheel of heaven’ (چرخ فلک = the heavens/fate). برنهی is an archaic 2nd-person verb form meaning ‘you lift up/produce/present’ (here in a prohibitory context: ‘do not present/raise an excuse before heaven’); it is a poetic, older conjugation and connects as the verb of this clause.
segments.s287-g113: This is a reported-speech frame: بدو 'to him' (indirect object) + گفت (said) + گودرز (the speaker's name). The vocative کای شیرمرد follows as the quoted address: کای is the archaic vocative 'O' and شیرمرد is the addressee epithet 'brave man'.
segments.s287-g114: نه ... نه ... is a correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. آزموده is a past participle 'tried/proven' governed by the preceding adjective گرم ('fervent/ardent'); ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the world' modifying آزموده. سرد is the second pole of the negation ('cold' or 'aloof').
segments.s287-g115: نبینی is a rhetorical second-person present negative ('do you not see') used to call attention; که introduces the content clause 'that we are ...'. ماییم is the present copula + pronoun 'we are', and پیروزگر is a predicate noun/adjective 'victorious' describing the speaker(s).
segments.s287-g116: بدین کار is an adverbial phrase 'to/for this work' (ب- + دین = 'with/for this' + کار 'task'). مشتاب is an imperative 'hurry!' in the second person singular. تند‌ای is a vocative/emphatic address 'O swift one' and پسر is the vocative 'son' (here an address meaning 'young man' or 'youth').
segments.s287-g117: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'over/on them'; بود is the past copula 'was'. The personal/poetic inversion places گستهم (here a name: Gostaham) after the copula, followed by the epithet چیره‌بخت 'fortunate/victorious' — literally 'Over them was Gostaham, the fortunate victor.'
segments.s287-g118: وز is here the fused prepositional sequence 'و ز' = 'and from'; ایشان 'them' is the source, and ستاند is a past transitive verb 'took (away)'. سرو، تاج و تخت are the coordinated direct objects (suro, crown and throne), listed after the verb.
segments.s287-g119: بمان is an imperative 'stay/remain'. تا کنون is a temporal clause 'until now/until I ...' that leads into the following purpose/temporal clause. از پس گستهم is a prepositional phrase 'after Gostaham' (in pursuit of Gostaham); here گستهم is a proper name (Gostaham), so the whole phrase says when/where the following sending will be directed.
segments.s287-g120: سواری فرستم is 'I will send a rider': سواری 'rider/horseman' is the object and فرستم is first-person singular present/subjunctive used here with a future sense 'I will send'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', شیر is 'lion', and دژم is an epithet 'grim/fierce' modifying شیر.
segments.s287-g145: This is a quoted clause followed by the reporting verb: برآرم is 1st-person present/future ('I will raise' / 'I lift') and appears as direct speech. همی گفت is an archaic/habitual past reporting phrase ('he said' / 'he kept saying'), so the clause before همی is the speech being reported. از + کوه + خاک = 'dust/from the mountain' (از = 'from', کوه modifies خاک: 'the mountain's dust').
segments.s287-g146: بدین جنگ جستن is an infinitival phrase meaning 'to take up / to seek this war' where ب- functions as a directional/prefixed preposition ('to/into') and جنگ جستن = 'seek/undertake war'. مرا زو چه باک is a colloquial poetic order: مرا = 'for me' (dative/ethical), زو = 'from it / because of it' (poetic contraction of از + او), and چه باک = 'what fear?' (exclamatory). The whole asks rhetorically what fear there is for me of entering this battle.
segments.s287-g147: کمر بست is a fixed past-tense expression 'he girded his loins' (کمر = waist/girdle, بست = bound/girded). و links the two actions. برساخت is a past transitive verb 'he made/raised/prepared' and takes جنگ را as its object; مر is the dative/ethical pronoun 'me / for me' placed before the object (poetic word order), so literally 'he prepared the war for me' but idiomatically 'he prepared for war'.
segments.s287-g148: به زین اندر آورد is a verb phrase meaning 'he brought/put onto the saddle' (به = 'to/onto', زین = 'saddle', اندر آورد = 'brought in/placed'). شبرنگ را is the definite object (را marks it); here شبرنگ is treated as the name/thing placed in the saddle (likely a horse's name), so the noun follows the verb phrase in poetic order.
segments.s287-g149: به گیو آگهی شد is a passive construction 'information came to Giv' (به = 'to', گیو = proper name, آگهی شد = 'was informed'). که introduces the subordinate clause. بیژن چو گرد begins the time clause: چو = 'when' and گرد here points forward to the following phrase (it will combine with کمر بست in the next segment).
segments.s287-g150: کمر بست is the main verb of the time-clause 'he girded his waist' (past). بر جنگ + فرشیدورد marks the direction/target of the action: بر جنگ = 'to/against the battle' (i.e. 'to go to war'), and فرشیدورد is the proper name associated with that battle (object/foe). The sequence reads 'when Bijan girded his loins, (he) went to fight Farshidvard.'
segments.s287-g151: پس = 'then/afterwards'. گستهم is an older/poetic verbal form indicating movement or dispersal (here best read as 'set forth' / 'were put in motion'); the phrase تازیان شد به راه uses شد as an auxiliary/past marker and places the subject (تازیان, 'the Tazians') after the verb cluster in inverted poetic order: 'then the Tazians took to the road.'
segments.s287-g152: به جنگ = 'to war' (directional). سواران توران سپاه is a compacted noun phrase in which سواران ('horsemen') and سپاه ('host/army') frame توران ('Turan') — read as 'the horsemen of Turan's host' or 'the cavalry of Turan's army'; poetic word order places the head noun (سپاه) at the end.
segments.s287-g177: This is an archaic prepositional + object phrase leading to the verb: ز بهر مرا = 'for my sake' (ز + بهر + مرا, with را split as a separate token here), followed by ز این سخن = 'from this speech'. بازگرد is an imperative/hortative verb 'turn away/withdraw' (literally 'return' in older diction) governing the preceding prepositional phrases.
segments.s287-g178: نشاید is an impersonal modal 'it is not fitting/it ought not (to be)'; که introduces the subordinate clause. داری is 2nd-person present 'you have/you cause' and here functions with the following phrase دل من به درد = 'my heart into pain' = 'make my heart ache'. Word order is somewhat inverted but reads: 'It is not fitting that you make my heart ache.'
segments.s287-g179: بدو گفت is a short classical sequence 'to him (بدو) said (گفت)' = 'said to him'. The vocative که‌ای (here functioning as a call) + پر خرد is a descriptive address: پر = 'full (of)' and خرد = 'wisdom', so 'O you full of wisdom' (a formal/vocative phrasing).
segments.s287-g180: جز این = 'except/other than this' is fronted. بر تو = 'about/with regard to you' (classical preposition + pronoun), مردم = subject 'people', and گمانی برد is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'to take/harbour a supposition / form an opinion'. The clause says essentially 'people take no opinion of you besides this.'
segments.s287-g181: که introduces a clause explaining or giving a reason. کار گذشته = 'the past deed(s) / past affair(s)'. بیاری here appears as a fused classical form meaning 'with help / by aid' (in manuscripts often written به یاری or بی‌یاری), and به یاد = 'in memory / remembered'. Together they mark that past deeds are kept/recallable (with/without aid) — the exact idiom is classical and compact.
segments.s287-g182: نپیچی is a negative imperative/subjunctive of پیچیدن 'turn' meaning 'do not turn (away)'. به خیره همی = 'in astonishment / staring' (به + خیره, with همی as an adverb 'still/continually'), سر ز داد = 'the head from justice' where ز = 'from' and داد = 'justice'. The clause warns: 'do not, amazed, turn your head away from justice.'
segments.s287-g183: بدان‌ای is the imperative 'know this' with the vocative 'ای پدر' = 'O father'. کین سخن = 'vengeful/hostile speech' (کین modifying سخن), and داد نیست is the predicate 'is not justice' (داد = 'justice'), so the clause asserts that such angry words are not justice.
segments.s287-g184: مگر here functions rhetorically ('isn't it that...? / surely?') to introduce a challenge or reminder. جنگ لاون = 'the battle of Lāvan' (place-name), ترا (written as the suffix ت + را here) = 'you' / 'to you', یاد نیست = 'is not remembered' i.e. 'you do not remember'. The whole line asks rhetorically whether he does not recall that battle.
segments.s287-g49: سپردی is 2nd-person singular past (you entrusted/assigned). مرا را is the object pronoun 'me' plus the object marker را (here marking/placing the experiencer/recipient). کوس و پرده‌سرای is the compound direct object (two things assigned to the speaker). Word order (verb before its object) is normal in this poetic register.
segments.s287-g50: به پیش سپه بر ببودن is a prepositional/infinitival phrase describing purpose or position: به پیش (before, in front of) + سپه (the army) + بر (toward/in front of) + ببودن (archaic infinitive of 'to be'). به پای at the end specifies manner/place ('on foot' / 'at the feet'). ببودن is a literary infinitive meaning 'to be/stand (there)'.
segments.s287-g51: دلیران همه (the brave, all) is the plural subject; نام جستند is past tense 3rd-person plural ('they sought/strived for a name/fame'). و connects to the following noun ننگ which continues the thought. In poetry the verb precedes or follows the subject fluidly; here the action (seeking fame) is foregrounded.
segments.s287-g52: مرا را is topical/experiencer marking ('as for me' / 'to me'); the clause is impersonal/experiential: بهره نآمد literally 'a share did not come' (i.e. I received no share). نآمد is past tense 'did not come', and به هنگام جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of battle'. The subject is omitted because the construction focuses on the outcome for 'مرا'.
segments.s287-g53: کنون من ('now I') is the temporal + subject. بدین کار is a literary compound (بدین = 'by/with this'; here split into prefix ب- + دین in the tokenization) meaning 'by this deed'. نام آورم uses آورم (1st-person present/subjunctive) in a literary sense to express coming-to-be of renown: 'I will gain/bring about a name' (i.e. earn renown).
segments.s287-g54: شومشان is a compact form: شوم (poetic/optative/present of 'become' or 'cause to be') with the clitic شان 'them' — here meaning 'I will bring misfortune on them / make them ill-omened'. یکایک ('one by one') is an adverbial; به دام آورم uses آورم (1st-person) with دام 'trap' to express 'I will bring them into the trap'. The two verbs form consecutive results: I will afflict them and then capture them individually.
segments.s287-g55: بخندید is past tense 'laughed' (verb often appears before the subject in the epic style). گودرز is the proper name subject. و + زو (here z + او contracted) means 'and from that/therefore' — ز (poetic از) marks 'from/because of', so و زو شاد شد = 'and because of it he became joyful.' شد is the past of شدن 'to become'.
segments.s287-g56: رخش تازه شد is literal: 'his steed (رخش) became fresh/new' with تازه شد (تازه + شد). وز غم آزاد شد is 'and from grief he was freed' where وز here functions as a combined poetic 'و + ز' = 'and from' linking to غم 'sorrow'; آزاد شد is passive/experiential 'was freed' or 'became free'. This clause describes resulting relief.
segments.s287-g193: بدو گفت گیو is a compact, somewhat inverted word order: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + گیو (Giv) = “Giv said to him.” ار introduces a conditional clause (‘if’). بگردی is a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning ‘you turn/return’ with the clitic subject تو; باز is an adverb ‘back/again.’ Together ار بگردی تو باز = “if you turn back.”
segments.s287-g194: همان is a demonstrative here ('that' / 'that very thing') and functions as the subject/pointed item of the comment. خوبتر is the comparative predicate ('better'). کین is a poetic/archaic item here and does not match the provided lexical senses (so indexed -1) — it functions as an intensifier/demonstrative linking to the following noun pair. نشیب و فراز is a fixed paired noun meaning ‘descent and ascent’ or 'ups and downs' and serves as the complement referenced by همان.
segments.s287-g195: تو بی‌من مپویی is a prohibitive/subjunctive addressed to the second person: تو (you) + بی‌من (without me) + مپویی (negative present/subjunctive/imperative 'do not go/seek'). به‌روز نبرد is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'on the day of battle' or 'in the battle-day' modifying the prohibition (i.e. 'do not go into battle without me').
segments.s287-g196: منت یار باشم is a promise formula: منت (favor/debt) + یار (companion/ally) with باشم (first-person present/subjunctive of بودن) meaning roughly 'I shall be/ I will be (in your debt)'. The provided glosses for باشم do not include this exact grammatical person/tense, so the verb is indexed -1. به هر کارکرد is a prepositional phrase 'for every/whatever deed' (به = for, هر = every/any, کارکرد = deed/performance) specifying what the speaker will be indebted for.
segments.s287-g197: بدو گفت بیژن follows the same inverted pattern as above: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + بیژن (Bijan) = 'Bijan said to him.' که here begins a subordinate clause expressing wish/negation. این خود مباد = 'may this very thing not be' where مباد is the classical optative/negative (may it not be), and خود intensifies 'این' ('this very').
segments.s287-g198: The continuation clause beginning with که ('that') completes the wish: از نامداران خسرونژاد is a genitival/partitive phrase 'from/amid the renowned ones of Khosrow's lineage' (از = from/of, نامداران = the famed/renowned, خسرونژاد = scions/offspring of Khosrow). Grammatically it supplies the feared source or agent in the subordinate clause introduced by که.
segments.s287-g199: سه گرد is a subject noun phrase 'three (bands/companies)' — گرد here is a martial/group noun not well captured by the provided senses, so indexed -1. از پی is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit/after'; بیم خورده is a past-participial adjective 'struck/possessed by fear' modifying the subject (خورده is the participle). تور is the proper name Tūr (here likely one or more persons named Tur).
segments.s287-g200: بتازند is a 3rd-person plural verb 'they rush/charge/attack' (present/subjunctive/epic present used to narrate action). پویان is a participial/epithet 'the swift/active ones' modifying the subject. بدین راه دور is an adverbial phrase 'along this distant road' (ب- as prepositional prefix + دین/this; note the split in the supplied word list makes دین appear as a separate lexical item but here functions as part of the adverbial 'بدین'). راه = road/way and دور = distant.
segments.s287-g185: که here introduces a subordinate (relative/explanatory) clause: 'that/what'. با من is a prepositional phrase 'with me' (با + object pronoun من). چه کرد is an interrogative/relative phrase 'what did (he) do' with کرد in the simple past; the subject is omitted (understood from context). اندر آن گستهم = اندر (poetic 'in/within') + آن 'that' + گستهم, a poetic first‑person form meaning 'my wandering' (the -م ending marks first person), so the whole phrase means 'in that wandering of mine.'
segments.s287-g186: غم و شادمانیش is a coordinated noun phrase 'his/her grief and rejoicing' (شادمانی + third‑person suffix -ش). با من به هم is an adverbial phrase: با من 'with me' and به هم 'together/combined' (poetic order), meaning that both his sorrow and his joy accompanied or were joined with me.
segments.s287-g187: ورایدون is a poetic past form meaning 'appeared/entered' (third person plural form used impersonally here); the clause asks کجا 'where' about گردش ایزدی 'the divine turning/wheel' (گردش as a noun 'turning, revolution', ایزدی 'divine' as postnominal adjective). Word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before the question word).
segments.s287-g188: فراز آورد is a verbal phrase 'brought up/caused to rise' where فراز (here adverbial/on high) combines with آورد (past 'bring/cause') to mean 'brought about' or 'produced'. روزگار بدی 'evil/bad fate' (روزگار 'fate/time', بدی 'evil/misfortune') is the result; overall: 'fate brought about misfortune.'
segments.s287-g189: نبشته here functions as 'that which is written / the writing' (a written text or inscription), and نگردد is a classical negative jussive/passive sense 'should not be made / ought not be turned into'. به پرهیز باز = 'as a warning/for caution again' (به + پرهیز 'for caution', باز 'again'). Together: 'let it not be written again as a caution.'
segments.s287-g190: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / it should not'; کشید here means 'to draw (out), to prolong' (infinitival sense following نباید). این سخن را دراز places the object: این سخن را 'this speech' (را marks the definite object) and دراز 'long' as the predicate/description — literally 'one should not draw this speech long.'
segments.s287-g191: ز (poetic from/away from) پیکار 'battle' + سر بر مگردان is an imperative negative construction: سر 'head' + بر 'away/on' + مگردان (negative imperative 'do not turn/return') — 'do not turn your head away from the fight.' که من introduces the following reason clause; the subject 'you' of the imperative is implied.
segments.s287-g192: فدی کرده دارم is a classical periphrasis: فدی (ransom/sacrifice) + کرده (perfect participle) + دارم (have) — 'I have made (it) a ransom' / 'I am ready to be sacrificed.' بدین کار تن is a prepositional/benefactive phrase: بدین (با + این 'with/for this') + کار 'work/affair' + تن 'body' — 'my body for this work.' The whole clause states willingness to give one's body for the cause.
segments.s287-g169: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نیکی‌دهش is a compound-like phrase: نیکی 'goodness' + دهش 'grant/gift' (i.e. 'the granting of good'), and بخت پیروز 'victorious fortune' is the agent/subject that precedes the past verb داد 'gave'. داد is a simple past (third person) 'gave'. The word order is poetic (modifier + noun + verb) but functions as a standard temporal clause 'When X gave...'.
segments.s287-g170: بباید is an impersonal modal 'one ought' / 'it is necessary' followed by the verbal noun/infinitive نشستن 'to sit' (here functioning as the complement expressing the required action). به آرام و شاد is a prepositional/manner phrase ('in/into calm and happy [state]') describing how one should sit; the coordinating و links the two adjectives.
segments.s287-g171: به پیش زمانه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence/face of Time' (پیش = 'before / in the presence of', زمانه = 'the age / the world'). چه is an exclamatory 'how' that introduces the rhetorical question, and تازی سرت addresses the addressee 'O Tazi (Arab), your head' (سرت = 'your head' used emphatically). The sequence is poetic but yields 'How, before time, O Arab, is your head...'.
segments.s287-g172: بس here is an interjection ('enough' / 'indeed' / 'how...') leading into the predicate. ایمن شدستی is a past-state expression: ایمن 'safe' as predicate adjective with شد (past of شدن) indicating 'became/are made safe' (second-person subject). بدین خنجرت is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/with this dagger' (بدین = 'by/with this', خنجرت = 'your dagger' / 'this dagger').
segments.s287-g173: کسی کو is an archaic/poetic relative 'someone who / he who' (کو functioning like 'که' = 'who'). بجوید is a present/simple-tense verb 'seeks' (generic/habitual), and سرانجام خویش is the object 'his own end/outcome' (سرانجام = 'end/outcome'; خوی + ش functions as خویش 'his own'). Word order places the verb after the relative phrase, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s287-g174: نجوید is the negative present/habitual 'does not seek / will not desire' (ن- prefix negates the verb). ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from the world' (ز = 'from'; گیتی = 'the world'); چنین کام خویش is the object 'such a desire/fulfillment of his' (چنین = 'such', کام = 'desire/wish', خوی + ش = 'his own'). The clause therefore states a general truth: someone seeking his ultimate end will not desire such worldly satisfaction.
segments.s287-g175: تو is the addressed 'you.' چندین here functions adverbially 'many times / so often / so much' and به گرد زمانه is 'around/about the world' (to pursue worldly affairs). مپوی is the negative imperative/command (م- + پیوی/پوییدن) 'do not run / do not go after / do not seek' directed to the second person.
segments.s287-g176: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause 'for / because.' او خود is emphatic 'he himself,' سوی ما is the directional 'toward us,' and نهادست is a poetic perfect/passive-like form meaning 'has set / has placed' (here 'has set his [face]'). روی 'face' completes the phrase: 'he himself has set/turned his face toward us.'
